items.q: Is flow production always the cheapest method per unit?
items.correct: Only at high, steady volumes — its large set-up costs need scale to spread; at low volumes or with varied products, batch or job production can be cheaper.
items.misconception: Flow production lowers unit costs for any business.
items.random_wrong: Flow production refers to manufacturing liquids only.
items.why: Students quote economies of scale without the volume condition attached.

items.q: Does mass customisation mean every product is handmade?
items.correct: No — it uses flexible production technology to offer customised options at near mass-production costs, combining scale with variety.
items.misconception: Customised products must always be made by job production.
items.random_wrong: Mass customisation means customers assemble products themselves in store.
items.why: The hybrid nature of mass customisation breaks the neat job/batch/flow boxes students memorise.

items.q: Is quality only the responsibility of inspectors?
items.correct: In a total quality management culture, every employee is responsible for quality at their own stage, aiming to prevent defects rather than catch them at the end.
items.misconception: Quality is checked by the quality department, so other workers needn't worry about it.
items.random_wrong: Quality control is outsourced to customers by law.
items.why: TQM's everyone-owns-quality principle contrasts with the inspection mindset students assume.

items.q: Car assembly lines use which method?
items.why: It's Flow. “Job” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Highest unit cost is typically in:
items.why: Correct answer: Job. “Batch” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 70. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
